text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Breast Medical Oncology — Surveillance Visit
Visit Type: Established Patient, Routine Surveillance (12 Months Post-Definitive Surgery)

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Breast Medical Oncology
PATIENT: Maria Elena Reyes
AGE: 46 years
DIAGNOSIS: Stage IIB (cT2N1M0, ypT1cN1M0) Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (TNBC), Left Breast, Grade 3; Invasive Ductal Carcinoma, NOS; Non-pCR after neoadjuvant pembrolizumab/AC/paclitaxel; RCB-II

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Reyes presents for her 12-month post-surgery surveillance visit. She has completed all planned neoadjuvant and adjuvant therapies and is now transitioning to active surveillance. She reports feeling well overall and is eager to discuss her ongoing monitoring plan and quality-of-life concerns.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Reyes is a 46-year-old premenopausal woman of Hispanic/Latina ethnicity, originally from Mexico, residing in the southwestern United States, who works as a high school teacher. She was diagnosed approximately 14 months ago with Stage IIB (cT2N1M0) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, upper outer quadrant, grade 3, with a Ki-67 of 85% and PD-L1 CPS of 18 by 22C3. She had no prior personal history of breast or gynecologic malignancy. Her germline BRCA1/2 testing was negative; an ATM p.V2424G variant of uncertain significance was identified on expanded panel.

She received neoadjuvant therapy per the KEYNOTE-522 schema: pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks plus dose-dense AC (doxorubicin 60 mg/m2/cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 q2 weeks x 4 cycles), followed by weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 x 12 doses with pembrolizumab q3 weeks continuing throughout. Total neoadjuvant duration was approximately 20 weeks. Paclitaxel was dose-reduced to 64 mg/m2 (20% reduction) for weeks 9-12 due to Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy. Pembrolizumab was held for one cycle during the paclitaxel phase for Grade 2 immune-related arthralgia affecting bilateral knees and wrists, managed with prednisone with successful resolution, and then resumed. She received 6 pembrolizumab doses during the neoadjuvant phase.

Post-neoadjuvant breast MRI showed a partial response with the left breast mass shrinking from 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm to approximately 1.4 x 1.1 x 0.9 cm. She subsequently underwent left partial mastectomy (breast-conserving surgery) with sentinel lymph node biopsy and completion axillary lymph node dissection. Final pathology confirmed ypT1cN1M0, with a 1.2 cm residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, at the left breast excision site; all margins were negative (closest margin 4 mm). Three of 16 axillary lymph nodes were positive for metastatic carcinoma with no extranodal extension. RCB score was 2.8 (RCB-II, intermediate residual disease). Miller-Payne grade 3 was assigned. Residual tumor was confirmed triple-negative (ER-negative, PR-negative, HER2 IHC 1+).

Given the non-pCR result, she received adjuvant capecitabine (Xeloda) 1000 mg/m2 twice daily x 14 days every 21 days, per the CREATE-X data, along with continuation of pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks to complete 1 year of total pembrolizumab therapy (17 doses total, neoadjuvant plus adjuvant). Adjuvant radiation was delivered to the left breast and regional lymphatics (whole breast 50 Gy in 25 fractions with a 10 Gy tumor bed boost; supraclavicular and axillary nodal irradiation) using IMRT, completed approximately 6 weeks after surgery.

During adjuvant therapy, she experienced Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) during Cycle 3 of capecitabine, prompting a 25% dose reduction to 750 mg/m2 twice daily for cycles 3-8. Grade 2 radiation dermatitis with moist desquamation was managed during RT. Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis was identified approximately 6 weeks after RT completion, treated with a brief course of low-dose prednisone and resolved without sequelae. At approximately month 7 post-surgery, she was found to have immune-mediated hypothyroidism (TSH 7.8 mIU/L) attributed to pembrolizumab, for which levothyroxine was initiated; the dose was uptitrated to 75 mcg daily and her thyroid function normalized. Pembrolizumab was continued throughout without interruption for this manageable endocrinopathy.

She completed her final (8th) cycle of capecitabine and the 17th pembrolizumab dose (completing 1 year of total pembrolizumab) approximately 2 months ago. A surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis and bilateral breast MRI performed at that time showed no evidence of local recurrence or distant metastasis; post-treatment changes only were noted. She now presents for her 12-month post-surgery clinic visit.

She is currently working full-time as a teacher and reports returning to most of her pre-diagnosis activities. She notes mild residual peripheral sensory symptoms in her fingertips (tingling, described as very mild) but states these are no longer limiting. She denies dyspnea, cough, chest pain, bone pain, headaches, visual changes, or neurologic symptoms. She has not noted any new breast masses, skin changes, or lymph node swelling. Her menstrual cycles have resumed following chemotherapy-associated amenorrhea, which resolved at approximately 9 months from initiation of chemotherapy. She reports improved energy and overall quality of life. She has been attending a peer support group for young women with breast cancer and is engaged with individual psychotherapy for cancer-related anxiety and body image concerns.

She is interested in discussing the possibility of future pregnancy and has been referred to reproductive endocrinology for counseling.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Improved energy overall; no fever, chills, night sweats, or significant unintentional weight change since completing therapy.
Pulmonary: Mild cough in the weeks following radiation, now fully resolved. No dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, or hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, lower extremity edema, or exertional dyspnea. She is aware of her history of anthracycline exposure.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite good. Normal bowel habits. Diarrhea from capecitabine fully resolved after completing therapy. No nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain.
Musculoskeletal: Immune-related arthralgia, previously Grade 2, now fully resolved. No joint pain, swelling, or stiffness. No new bone pain.
Neurological: Bilateral fingertip and toe tingling, Grade 0-1 at most, significantly improved from peak Grade 2 during paclitaxel. Denies new weakness, headaches, or focal neurological deficits.
Dermatologic: Hand-foot syndrome resolved. Scalp hair regrowth noted — she describes substantial regrowth, though hair texture slightly different. Skin at left breast/axilla healing well post-RT.
Endocrine: No symptoms of hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation). On levothyroxine 75 mcg daily with stable thyroid function.
Genitourinary: Menstrual cycles resumed at approximately month 9. Cycles currently regular (approximately every 28-32 days), though she reports some cycle variability. No dysuria, hematuria.
Psychosocial: Reports ongoing anxiety related to fear of recurrence, actively managed with therapy. No active depression per patient report. Body image concerns improving. Good social support from family, peer group, and therapeutic relationship.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
(As previously documented; summarized here)
- Stage IIB TNBC, left breast, diagnosed at age 45 — as detailed above
- Immune-mediated hypothyroidism, secondary to pembrolizumab — on levothyroxine
- No prior history of breast or ovarian cancer prior to index diagnosis
- Chemotherapy-associated amenorrhea, resolved
- No significant cardiac history; no hypertension, diabetes, or other chronic conditions

SURGICAL HISTORY
- Left partial mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy and axillary lymph node dissection
- Prior core needle biopsies (left breast and left axillary lymph node, at time of diagnosis)

SOCIAL HISTORY
- High school teacher
- Born in Mexico; resides in the southwestern United States
- Never-smoker
- Occasional alcohol prior to diagnosis; abstinent during treatment
- BMI 26.4 at diagnosis; currently estimated 25.8 (minor weight loss during chemotherapy with some recovery)
- Two children; interested in possible future third pregnancy (to be discussed with reproductive endocrinology)
- Good social and family support; engaged with peer support group and individual psychotherapy

FAMILY HISTORY
- Maternal aunt: breast cancer diagnosed at age 52
- Maternal cousin: recently diagnosed with ovarian cancer (new since prior documentation) — prompting referral for updated germline genetic counseling with expanded panel
- No paternal family history of breast, ovarian, pancreatic, or colorectal cancer documented

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies
- No contrast allergy on record (tolerated IV contrast with prior CT scans without incident)

MEDICATIONS
1. Levothyroxine (Synthroid) 75 mcg orally once daily in the morning (immune-mediated hypothyroidism)
2. Vitamin D3 2000 IU daily
3. Calcium carbonate 600 mg twice daily (initiated for bone health given premenopausal status with prior chemotherapy and ongoing surveillance)
4. Multivitamin daily
5. All chemotherapy, pembrolizumab (Keytruda), capecitabine (Xeloda), and radiation completed; no active oncologic treatment

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 116/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 14/min, temperature 98.2°F, weight 62.1 kg, height 163 cm, BMI 23.4 (recalculated at today's visit).
ECOG Performance Status: 0 (fully active, able to carry on all pre-disease activities without restriction; working full-time).

General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Hair regrowth visible — moderate-length scalp hair, slightly different texture than prior per patient. Appears well and in good health.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic. Mucous membranes moist. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No oral lesions.

Neck: Supple. No cervical, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No thyromegaly or thyroid nodularity on palpation.

Chest/Breast:
- Left breast: Well-healed curvilinear lumpectomy scar at the lateral upper outer quadrant. Some firmness at the scar site with mild skin thickening and retraction consistent with surgical and radiation changes. No palpable mass, no skin breakdown, no erythema or warmth suggesting infection. No skin satellite nodules. No nipple discharge.
- Right breast: No masses, no skin changes, no nipple discharge. Essentially normal on exam.
- Left axilla: Post-ALND changes with mild lymphedema of the left upper extremity (soft pitting edema at the dorsum of the left hand and mild puffiness of the left forearm). No new palpable adenopathy in the left axilla. Right axilla: normal, no lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema of lower extremities.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, crackles, or rhonchi. Breathing unlabored.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly on palpation. No palpable masses. Normal bowel sounds. No ascites.

Extremities: Mild left upper extremity lymphedema as noted. No lower extremity edema. No clubbing or cyanosis.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Sensation intact bilaterally in lower extremities to light touch; minor residual decreased sensation at bilateral fingertips, symmetric, minimal — consistent with near-complete resolution of paclitaxel-induced peripheral neuropathy. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Gait normal.

Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling or tenderness of wrists, knees, or other joints. Full range of motion throughout. Immune-related arthralgia fully resolved.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.8 x 10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 12.4 g/dL (mildly low; slight decrease from prior, likely ongoing mild recovery from chemotherapy-related anemia; previously stable in range)
- Platelets: 218 x 10^9/L (normal)
- ANC: 3.6 x 10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride 102 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L
- BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60, normal)
- AST 22 U/L, ALT 18 U/L (normal liver function)
- Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL
- Alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L (normal)
- Albumin 4.1 g/dL

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 2.3 mIU/L (within normal range, 0.4-4.0 mIU/L) — well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

Lipid panel ordered today (baseline given anthracycline exposure and cardiovascular risk counseling — results pending at time of dictation).

Tumor markers: CA 27-29 and CEA — not routinely drawn per NCCN/ASCO guidance as not standard for surveillance in non-metastatic breast cancer. Not ordered.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Surveillance CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (obtained at end of adjuvant therapy, approximately 2 months prior to this visit):
- CT chest: No new pulmonary nodules. Mild post-radiation changes in the left lung base, appearing as a subtle ground-glass opacity, smaller than on prior imaging — consistent with resolving radiation pneumonitis. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Left axillary surgical bed with post-surgical changes; no new adenopathy.
- CT abdomen: No hepatic lesions. No suspicious retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Liver, spleen, adrenal glands, kidneys, and pancreas all unremarkable.
- CT pelvis: No pelvic adenopathy or suspicious osseous lesions on bone windows.
- Impression: No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. Post-treatment changes only.

Bilateral breast MRI with and without contrast (obtained concurrently with CT, approximately 2 months prior to this visit):
- Left breast: Expected post-operative and post-radiation signal change at the lumpectomy site. No suspicious new or residual enhancement. No mass or concerning architectural distortion beyond known post-treatment changes.
- Right breast: No suspicious enhancement, no masses.
- Bilateral axillae: No pathologic adenopathy identified.
- Impression: No evidence of local recurrence. Stable post-treatment changes.

Echocardiogram (baseline cardiac surveillance, ordered in setting of prior doxorubicin exposure — performed during this visit period):
- LVEF: 62% (normal systolic function)
- No wall motion abnormalities. Normal valvular function. No pericardial effusion.
- Impression: Normal echocardiogram. No evidence of anthracycline-related cardiotoxicity at this time.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Reyes is a 46-year-old premenopausal woman with Stage IIB (ypT1cN1M0) triple-negative breast cancer of the left breast, treated with neoadjuvant pembrolizumab/AC/paclitaxel followed by breast-conserving surgery and ALND (non-pCR, RCB-II), adjuvant radiation, adjuvant capecitabine, and pembrolizumab to complete 1 year of therapy. All planned treatment has been completed. She is currently 12 months post-surgery and presents today in excellent condition with no evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant metastasis.

1. SURVEILLANCE — NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE (NED)
Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bilateral breast MRI are reassuring, with no signs of disease recurrence. Clinically she is well, ECOG PS 0, back to full activity and work. The left breast exam shows expected post-treatment changes without any concerning findings. No lymphadenopathy. Will continue the following surveillance plan:
   - Clinical visits with exam and labs every 3-4 months for the next year (months 12-24 post-surgery), then every 6 months thereafter through 5 years.
   - Annual bilateral breast MRI and diagnostic mammography per NCCN high-risk guidelines, given age < 50 and TNBC diagnosis.
   - The first annual mammogram following lumpectomy is scheduled and the patient has been given the order.
   - Bone health: Given her prior chemotherapy with anthracyclines and taxanes as well as potential transient ovarian suppression, a baseline DEXA scan is ordered today to assess bone mineral density. Will review results and counsel on calcium, vitamin D, and weight-bearing exercise per ASCO guidelines for breast cancer survivors.

2. IMMUNE-MEDIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM
Well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH today is 2.3 mIU/L — within goal range. She is asymptomatic. Will continue current dose. Repeat TSH at next visit (approximately 3-4 months). Lifelong monitoring recommended given pembrolizumab-related thyroid dysfunction is typically permanent.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (POST-PACLITAXEL)
Significantly improved; Grade 0-1 at most, with only very mild residual fingertip tingling. No functional limitation. This is consistent with expected late recovery trajectory following dose reduction during the paclitaxel phase. No specific treatment required at this time. Will reassess at each visit. Majority of patients with this degree of residual neuropathy at 12 months continue to improve over the following year.

4. LEFT UPPER EXTREMITY LYMPHEDEMA
Mild post-ALND lymphedema of the left upper extremity noted on exam. Referred to lymphedema therapy (certified lymphedema therapist) today for formal assessment and fitting for compression garment as appropriate. Patient educated on skin care, avoidance of blood draws and blood pressure cuffs on the left arm, wound care, and lymphedema precautions. Will incorporate ongoing monitoring at each visit.

5. CARDIOVASCULAR SURVEILLANCE (POST-ANTHRACYCLINE)
Baseline echocardiogram today shows LVEF of 62%, normal — reassuring. No evidence of cardiotoxicity. She received doxorubicin at 60 mg/m2 x 4 cycles (cumulative dose approximately 240 mg/m2), which is below the typical threshold for clinically significant cardiotoxicity but warrants ongoing surveillance. Will repeat echocardiogram in 2-3 years or sooner if symptoms develop. Encouraged aerobic exercise, heart-healthy diet, and avoidance of tobacco.

6. BONE HEALTH
DEXA scan ordered today. Patient is premenopausal with resumption of menstrual cycles (cycles now regular), which is encouraging. She is already taking calcium 600 mg twice daily and vitamin D3 2000 IU daily per prior guidance. Will review DEXA results and adjust supplementation or consider bisphosphonate if osteopenia or osteoporosis is identified.

7. REPRODUCTIVE CONCERNS AND OVARIAN FUNCTION
Menstrual cycles resumed at approximately 9 months after initiation of chemotherapy, suggesting ovarian function recovery — this is a favorable sign. She is interested in the possibility of a future pregnancy. Referral to reproductive endocrinology placed today for evaluation of ovarian reserve (AMH, antral follicle count) and comprehensive counseling on pregnancy safety following TNBC treatment. Current data suggest that pregnancy after TNBC, particularly with adequate disease-free interval (typically at least 2 years post-treatment is often discussed), does not appear to worsen oncologic outcomes, though individualized counseling is essential. This is to be discussed further with reproductive endocrinology and confirmed with updated literature.

8. GENETIC COUNSELING
Patient's maternal cousin was recently diagnosed with ovarian cancer — this new family history warrants updated genetic counseling. A referral to genetic counseling has been placed for re-evaluation with expanded germline panel (including BRCA1/2, PALB2, RAD51C, RAD51D, BRIP1, ATM re-evaluation in the context of the VUS, and additional panel genes). Current testing showed ATM p.V2424G — a variant of uncertain significance — and otherwise negative panel. Given the new second-degree relative with ovarian cancer, further counseling is appropriate. Potential implications for the patient's children will also be discussed.

9. PSYCHOSOCIAL
She is engaged with individual therapy and a peer support group, which is excellent. Fear of recurrence is common and expected, particularly at the 12-month mark following active treatment completion. Encouraged continued psychotherapy and peer support engagement. Referred for ongoing support through the institutional survivorship program. Social work consulted if needed. Discussed the concept of the "new normal" and the ongoing adjustment that follows completion of cancer treatment.

10. PATIENT EDUCATION AND COUNSELING
Discussed surveillance rationale, signs and symptoms warranting urgent evaluation (new breast lump, persistent bone pain, dyspnea, neurologic changes, unexplained weight loss), and the importance of attendance at regular follow-up appointments. She verbalized understanding. Discussed healthy lifestyle interventions — regular aerobic exercise (goal 150 minutes moderate activity/week), Mediterranean diet pattern, healthy body weight maintenance, alcohol minimization — all with evidence for improving outcomes in breast cancer survivors. Given her excellent ECOG PS 0 and return to full activity, strongly encouraged maintenance of an active lifestyle.

REFERRALS PLACED TODAY:
- Lymphedema therapy (certified lymphedema therapist)
- Reproductive endocrinology (ovarian reserve evaluation, pregnancy counseling)
- Genetic counseling (updated germline panel given new family history)

ORDERS:
- DEXA scan (baseline bone mineral density)
- Fasting lipid panel (results pending)
- Annual mammogram (left breast and right breast) — order given to patient
- TSH repeat at next visit
- Echocardiogram follow-up in 2-3 years (or sooner per symptoms)

RETURN VISIT: In approximately 3-4 months for next surveillance visit.

Time spent counseling patient: approximately 50 minutes. Greater than 50% of visit time spent in discussion of prognosis, disease surveillance, and health maintenance counseling.

---

DICTATED AND REVIEWED BY: Breast Medical Oncology Attending
CC: Primary Care Physician, Surgical Oncology, Radiation Oncology, Endocrinology
